Mined-and-confirmed terminal threshold. When non-null and a tracked
record's

reaches this value, the
record transitions to terminal (

The retention countdown then begins from that block.

Pre-v0.15, normally-mined transactions never reached terminal —
retention enforcement only fired on replacement or
unseen-for-N-blocks paths, so successful txs accumulated in
long-lived stores forever.

(default) preserves that
behavior; opt in with a value

to safely
retire mined records past the reorg window.

Validation: must be

(default reorg depth = 12) to
avoid premature terminal during a same-height reorg unmining
the tx.
Optional logger callback. Same shape as

.
The tracker calls it at decision points the consumer might want
to surface: rehydration counts, dedup-migration writes, terminal
transitions, retention-expiry firings, capability-degradation /
recovery transitions. Errors continue to flow through

;
the logger covers the "what did the tracker decide" question.
How many blocks past a terminal-and-finalized state (

emitted) before the tracker drops a
record and emits

(spec §10). Pass the same value to your store
implementation so persisted retention matches in-memory.
Records still in flight (no terminal observation) are not subject
to retention; they live until their last subscriber leaves AND
they have no durable subscription (cleanupRecord path).
Cadence for per-hash status polling via

). Runs once
per block tick for every tracked record by default —

disables the path entirely. The
status-poll path is the consumer-friendly default for chains where

is gated or where mempool gossip is unreliable
(PulseChain et al):

is universally exposed
and queries the node's indexed-tx store, so it sees txs that aren't
currently in the local pool but the node has seen referenced.

Cost: one

RPC per tracked record per
matching tick. Override per-subscription via

when the consumer has a
better-visibility per-hash check than the default RPC method.

Per-member TrackOptions applied to each hash.
Per-hash selector — one tracked hash, ergonomic for wallet UIs.

Tunable knobs for the default in-memory store.

-

(default 64): how many blocks past the last
  terminal observation a record stays in the store. After this
  window passes, the record is GC'd. Block-units, not seconds —
  reorg safety is a block-depth invariant, not a time invariant
  (spec §10.1).

-

(default 256): bounded ring buffer cap on
  the per-hash audit log. Older events are dropped when the cap
  is exceeded; the latest status is always retained.

summary.text:
Per-hash and per-bulk subscription metadata. Predicate selectors
are non-serializable (closures cannot survive a process boundary)
— the tracker silently demotes them to non-durable and surfaces a
warning at registration. Per spec §13.2 only
bulk selectors and per-hash selectors persist meaningfully.
Whether the consumer asked for durable persistence.
Stable identifier. The tracker assigns this on registration.

carries every persisted (durable) subscription that referenced this
hash so
Block number of the very first observation.
Block number of the most recent observation (any kind).

Block number after which the record is GC-eligible. Set when the
record reaches a terminal observation (
Cached current status, kept in sync with every emit.

spec §5.4.

consumers set this true.

Capability gate: requires source.capabilities().receiptByHash ===
'available'; when unavailable, events still flow but
is absent and a one-shot warning surfaces via onError.
Mined-and-confirmed terminal — fires when an included tx's

threshold
configured on the tracker. Marks the record as terminal for
retention purposes (the existing terminal anchors are
replacement and unseen-for-N-blocks; v0.15 closes the gap where a
normally-mined tx never goes terminal and leaks records in
long-lived stores). Carries the confirmations count that triggered
the transition so consumers can gate downstream UI cleanup off the
same event. Fires exactly once per record — the

gate on the decision arm dedups
subsequent confirmation bumps.
A different hash with the same

pair was either
seen in the mempool or mined.

when the replacement was only observed in the mempool; it
carries the mined block's number when the replacement reached
inclusion before the original.

counts blocks observed since this inclusion
inclusive of the inclusion block itself (so the first
inclusion event has
is present iff the subscription set

. Reorg.

Per spec §12.3, this event never carries

— receipt-poll cannot detect a reorg
because most providers happily return a receipt for a tx in a
no-longer-canonical block.

All members of the group have reached inclusion in the canonical
chain and none have been replaced or reorged. Final progress event
before group stream closes (unless a reorg or replacement occurs).

Common envelope on every group event. Carries the group identifier
and the block coordinate the observation was made at.

One member of the group was dropped (unseen for the threshold),
failed (reverted or invalid), or replaced by a different tx with
the same (from, nonce) pair.

Emitted when one or more members of the tracked group have reached
inclusion in the canonical chain.

is the count of members
observed in blocks;

is the most
recently-included member's hash.

Group subscription was stopped before all members reached inclusion.
Fires once per group subscription. Always the final event in the
stream for that group.

to tear down all member
subscriptions.

— cached snapshot the state machine maintains per

returns this; the iterator and

verb. Consumers interpret the fields as policy.

is called or the tracker stops.

Stop the bulk subscription. Per-hash subscriptions auto-tracked
via this bulk subscription continue under their own retention
rules (spec §11.1).

Imperative subscription to per-hash events on every matched tx.
Returns an unsubscribe handle.

Cross-tx correlation — track a logical group of related hashes
(e.g., a wallet's "claim + swap" pair). Emits group-level
synthesis events derived from the per-member event streams.
See spec §18.1, v0.8.0 design F3.

Promise that resolves when durable-subscription rehydration
triggered by the most recent

has completed. For
in-memory stores this typically resolves on the next microtask;
for cross-process restart with Redis / SQLite / etc, this is the
gate indexer / relay consumers should

before assuming
the tracked-set is fully restored:

  tracker.start()
  await tracker.ready()
  // safe to begin processing — durable records from previous run
  // are now registered against the source.

Returns an already-resolved promise when

has not been
called or the previous rehydration already finished. Resolves to
— errors during rehydration are routed through

and don't reject this promise (one bad store call shouldn't
crash consumer flow that's waiting for ready).

Persistence surface. Indexers and relays cannot lose tracked hashes
across restart; wallets are fine in-memory.

is
optional — durable stores that don't keep a log can omit it.

Append an event to the per-hash audit log. Indexers replay this
log on restart; wallets can wrap a no-op store implementation.
Failures here are routed through the tracker's

and
never block live emit (per spec Appendix A).

Remove a hash. Called when the retention window expires.

**Contract: implementations must clear ALL state associated with
the hash** — the record itself, the event log (if any), and any
other per-hash keys the implementation maintains. A common bug in
custom stores is forgetting to delete the event log alongside the
record, leaving orphaned log entries that never expire. The
first-party

already enforce this; consumer
implementations must do the same.
Read the latest record for a hash. Returns null if absent.

List records that carry at least one durable subscription. Called
once at

so durable per-hash subscriptions can
be re-registered against the source after a restart.
Persist (or update) a tracked-tx record. Idempotent on

Read the per-hash audit log, optionally constrained to events
with

. Optional — implementations
without a log return

on the type and get pruned
from the catch-up code path.

watchers into one stream should set this for unambiguous routing.

in any mempool within this many block ticks. Default 12.

Blocks of "no observation" before 'dropped' resolves. Default 12.

If true, fetches the receipt at inclusion and resolves with
'failed' when receipt.status === '0x0'. Adds one RPC.

Required confirmations before onMined fires. Default 1.
Pass through to the internal ChainSource.
Blocks of "no observation" before onDropped fires. Default 12.

Consumer-supplied mined-detection probe. Attached per-subscription via

. The tracker dispatches it for the tracked
hash on every block tick, in addition to its own block-poll inclusion check.

  - Return

when the probe can't confirm inclusion. Probe throws are
    routed through

and treated as null.
  - Whichever path — block-poll or probe — reports a strictly newer
    inclusion wins; the existing height-ordering rule prevents the
    lower-authority arrival from clobbering the higher.

The probe is NOT permitted to drive reorg / vanished-from-block events
(spec §12.3) — divergence detection stays anchored on the source's block
stream where the parent-hash chain is authoritative.

Default per-tracker bulk-subscription cap (§11.3). Higher fan-out
is technically allowed but indicates the consumer should be
running an indexer-shaped store rather than the in-memory default.

Default reorg-detection depth in blocks. Conservative — even
Ethereum's worst recent reorgs are under 7 blocks, and unbounded
walks would let a single anomalous reorg make the tick arbitrarily
long (§12.2). Tunable per-tracker via

Default retention window in blocks — exposed so the tracker can
use the same default if no store-level override is present.

Append a freshly-observed canonical block into a bounded ring
keyed by block number, capped at

entries. Returns
a new ring (input is not mutated). The newest entry overwrites
any previous entry at the same number — a same-height reorg
replaces the stale block with the new canonical one.

Snapshot constructor for a freshly-tracked hash. Used by the
tracker to seed its in-memory record before any observation has
landed. All "last observed" fields start

for this kind — receipt-poll cannot
detect a reorg authoritatively, so the builder rejects that
combination at construction rather than letting a malformed
event ship to consumers.

returns the
caller's function as-is.

Throws on a malformed selector (

without a function) — caught at registration time
rather than at the per-tick callsite, where the failure mode would
be silent zero matches.

Compute the retention-expiry block for a terminal observation.
Pure helper exposed so the tracker (which decides when to call

) and
the store implementations stay in sync on the calculation.
Default in-memory store. Synchronous internals wrapped in

; safe for any consumer that doesn't need
cross-process durability.

. See module docstring
for the key layout and serialization details.
Create a multi-chain coordinator over one

per chain.

The registered chain set is fixed at construction — a coordinator
with mutable membership would need rules for orphaned subscriptions
that the thin-multiplexer design deliberately avoids. Construct a
new coordinator when the chain set changes.

summary.text: Construct a group subscription that synthesises per-member
Member transaction hashes. Order is not significant.

.
Exported so consumers can run prefix cleanup without instantiating
the full store — e.g., on app boot before constructing the tracker.

sequence (the tracker's
latest observation, plus any walk-back probes it performed). At
each height present in BOTH sides, if the hashes disagree the
detector returns a

are skipped** — the detector
treats "no canonical entry at this height" as "no information,"
not "vanished." A real same-height reorg requires the caller to
explicitly pass the new canonical block at that height; gapping
(e.g. caller skipped a height) is not a divergence signal.

If you need to detect "ring's tip is no longer in canonical" you
pass the canonical chain that explicitly covers that height; with
a partial canonical sequence the detector stays conservative.

caps how far back the comparison runs. The tracker
rarely cares about divergences beyond

because
any tracked tx that deep would already be considered finalized by
downstream consumer policy.

Returns an empty array on a clean chain extension (no divergences).
against every compiled selector and yield one

per (tx, selector) match. Used by the tracker's
per-tick fan-out over both

(after a new tip
lands) and the source's mempool snapshot deltas.

The tracker, not this helper, decides whether to auto-track each
matched hash —

Submit a same-nonce replacement transaction with bumped gas.
Returns the new tx hash. Throws whatever the wallet client throws
(no swallowing — caller decides retry / surface).

content.text:
```ts
import { replaceTransaction } from '@valve-tech/tx-tracker'

  const newHash = await replaceTransaction({
    original: { to: '0xrecipient', nonce: 42, data: '0x...', value: 0n },
    walletClient,
    newGas: bumpForReplacement(currentGas), // from @valve-tech/gas-oracle
  })
```

summary.text:
Watch a single tx hash. Returns an unsubscribe function — calling
it before terminal cancels the watch and tears down the internal
source/tracker.

content.text:
```ts
const stop = watchTransaction({
    client,
    hash: '0xabc...',
    confirmations: 3,
    onMined: (event) => console.log('mined at', event.blockNumber),
    onDropped: () => console.log('dropped'),
  })
  // ... later, before terminal
  stop()
```
